design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate grid-based maze levels, eliminate Nazi enemies with escalating firepower, reach elevator to progress. Collect keys, health, ammo, and treasures while managing resources.
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (ammo, health) versus enemy density and patrol patterns. Risk-reward of exploring secret areas versus advancing to exit.
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Allied spy escaping Nazi Castle and conducting covert military operations against overwhelming fascist forces. Progression from knife-wielding captive to gun-wielding commando.
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Deliberately shifted from stealth (original Castle Wolfenstein) to fast-paced, violent action. Designed to be 'fast and violent, unlike other computer games on the market at the time.' Established FPS as high-speed action genre rather than tactical simulator. Technical prowess and speed prioritized over narrative depth.

difficulty.lives_system: Player begins each episode with four lives. Health represented as percentage (0-100). Losing all health costs one life and restarts current level with minimal loadout (knife, pistol, eight bullets). Extra-life tokens and point thresholds grant additional lives.
difficulty.continues_system: Unlimited continues; player restarts level upon death with progression through episodes preserved.
difficulty.checkpointing: Original DOS version allows saving at any point. Many ports restrict saving between levels only.
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: No explicit difficulty settings documented. Point-scoring system incentivizes optional challenge (kill all enemies, collect all treasure, find all secrets, beat par time), creating self-directed difficulty scaling. Secret areas reward exploration with resources or shortcuts.

progression.upgrade_system: Weapon progression through pickup placement: knife → pistol → submachine gun → chain gun. Each weapon tier increases damage and fire rate. Health refills and ammo scattered to sustain progression.
progression.unlock_mechanism: Colored keys unlock corresponding colored doors, gating level geography. Episode structure (10 levels per episode, final level is boss encounter) provides pacing gates.
replayability_hooks: Point scoring system tracks enemy kills, treasure collection, secret area discovery, and time-to-completion with displayed ratios
Optional secret areas hidden behind activatable wall tiles create exploration incentives
Par time challenges encourage speedrunning optimization

world_building_techniques: Episode titles establish narrative arc: 'Escape from Castle Wolfenstein' → 'Operation: Eisenfaust' → 'Die, Führer, Die!' → 'The Nocturnal Missions'
Level theming: Nazi bunkers and buildings reinforce setting and context
Enemy variety (Nazi guards, soldiers, dogs) builds world through combat encounters
Sprite artwork and environment design convey WWII aesthetic

boss_design.philosophy: Episode climax encounters distinguish final levels as threshold moments. Boss fights serve as narrative payoffs to progression rather than integrated gameplay challenge.
notable_bosses: Final bosses anchor each episode's conclusion (specific names not documented in sources)

faction_indicators: Enemy faction: Nazi Germany (guards, soldiers, command structure implied)
danger_signals: Nazi imagery and bunker architecture establish threat environment
Enemy patrol patterns and density create ambient danger
environmental_storytelling: Castle Wolfenstein setting evokes historical WWII context
Grid-based maze design suggests bunker complexity and disorientation
Treasure placement implies Nazi wealth and resources
Locked doors and key requirements suggest secure military infrastructure
